retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Stable coronary artery disease is a risk factor, though patients with silent myocardial ischemia may or may not have coronary artery disease. Individuals with this condition need an aggressive diagnostic and therapeutic approach due to the increased risk of developing new coronary events compared to people with clinically detected ischemia. The presence of silent ischemia is a strong mortality predictor.

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Silent myocardial ischemia's most common causes include atherosclerotic plaque rupture, coronary vasospasm, coronary vasculitis, trauma, coronary embolism, sympathomimetic drugs like cocaine, and supply-demand mismatch. The associated risk factors play a significant role in causation. The following risk factors increase susceptibility to asymptomatic ischemic episodes and are pertinent to developing silent myocardial ischemia:

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A 1-mm downsloping or horizontal ST depression is suggestive of myocardial ischemia in patients with previously normal ECG. Cardiac biomarkers like troponin-I and ambulatory ECG monitoring help detect postoperative myocardial infarction. These modalities may also help screen older individuals and patients with DM presenting to the hospital for unrelated complaints but are at risk of CAD. Radionuclide imaging is instrumental in detecting ischemia in asymptomatic individuals. The following combination of tests is used to detect silent myocardial ischemia. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Screen patients at risk for silent myocardial ischemia. Differentiate between silent and symptomatic myocardial ischemia based on clinical presentation and diagnostic test results. Develop a personalized management strategy for a patient diagnosed with silent myocardial ischemia. Implement effective collaboration and communication protocols within an interprofessional team caring for patients with silent myocardial ischemia to optimize outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Etiology -- Older Patients
retrieved_docs.content: Older patients are particularly susceptible to silent myocardial ischemia. A study of 678 healthy men and women with no prior atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease revealed that the prevalence of silent myocardial ischemia, detected by Holter monitoring, was 11.4%. Silent ischemia in this population was associated with 3 times the risk of adverse cardiovascular events after adjusting for other risk factors. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosing silent myocardial ischemia presents challenges due to the absence of noticeable symptoms, often requiring incidental detection during routine medical evaluations or through diagnostic tests such as electrocardiograms (ECGs), stress testing, or coronary angiography. Despite the absence of symptoms, silent myocardial ischemia elevates the risk of serious complications such as myocardial infarction, heart failure, and sudden cardiac death. Screening individuals with CAD risk factors is essential to detect silent myocardial ischemia early.

retrieved_docs.title: Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome Antiretroviral Therapy(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Acute HIV infection may manifest as a mononucleosis-like syndrome with mainly nonspecific symptoms or be completely asymptomatic. Chronic, untreated infection progresses to CD-4 cell depletion and the development of acquired immunodeficiency syndrome with the appearance of different opportunistic infections and metabolic, cardiovascular, oncologic, and neurocognitive comorbidities.

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Silent myocardial ischemia is a condition characterized by reduced blood flow to the myocardium without causing noticeable symptoms like chest pain or discomfort. This condition's asymptomatic nature makes it particularly dangerous, as individuals may not be aware of the underlying heart condition until complications arise. The risk factors for silent myocardial ischemia align closely with those for symptomatic CAD, including hypertension, diabetes, smoking, hypercholesterolemia, obesity, lack of physical activity, and a family history of heart disease. Mechanistically, silent myocardial ischemia arises from processes similar to symptomatic ischemia, such as reduced blood flow to the heart due to coronary arterial obstruction, often from atherosclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity for healthcare professionals is designed to enhance learners' competence in identifying patients with silent myocardial ischemia and managing the condition. Participants gain a deeper understanding of the nuanced diagnostic and treatment approaches to this condition, enabling them to contribute meaningfully within an interprofessional team caring for patients with silent myocardial ischemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of silent ischemia is a strong mortality predictor. About 70% to 80% of transient ischemic episodes lack anginal chest symptoms (silent ischemia). Lack of pain in silent myocardial ischemia increases morbidity and mortality since patients do not seek medical treatment in a timely fashion. [2] [3] Older patients with diabetes mellitus (DM) and those with prior myocardial infarction or revascularization history are susceptible.

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Etiology -- Perioperative Myocardial Infarction
retrieved_docs.content: The geriatric population has a relatively high perioperative myocardial infarction incidence. Studies have shown that patients who underwent coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG) had episodes of silent myocardial ischemia detected by Holter monitoring. Perioperative silent myocardial ischemia is usually asymptomatic. Hence, ECG monitoring is recommended during this period. Risk factors like prior myocardial infarction history and the presence of peripheral artery disease increase the odds of perioperative myocardial infarction. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The history of present illness and physical examination provide an immediate source of information for clinicians to stratify the patients for further workup. Pertinent comorbidities include DM, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and CAD or the presence of other CAD risk factors. The physical examination may be benign in these patients or suggest signs of ventricular dysfunction, such as S3, or ischemia, such as S4.

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: In contrast, silent myocardial ischemia is asymptomatic. However, chest pain and respiratory distress may arise if silent myocardial ischemia develops into an acute myocardial infarction. A thorough clinical history and judicious diagnostic evaluation can help differentiate silent myocardial ischemia from these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1513
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي is a vascular condition that is clinically associated with several observable signs and health considerations. It is linked to the presence of a systolic murmur (النفخة الانقباضية), indicating turbulent blood flow during cardiac contraction, and to arterial bruits such as a carotid bruit (لغط الشريان السباتي) and a femoral bruit (لغط فخذي), which reflect turbulent flow in the carotid and femoral arteries respectively. Patients with التصلب العصيدي may also present with a limp (عرج), suggesting compromised limb perfusion or musculoskeletal involvement, and the condition emphasizes the importance of routine health maintenance (حفظ الصحة الروتيني) to monitor and manage cardiovascular risk. Additionally, التصلب العصيدي is associated with ذَبْحَةُ برنزميتال, a specific clinical or pathological finding that further characterizes the disease spectrum. The condition includes a more extensive form known as التصلب العصيدي المعمم, representing a generalized manifestation of arterial hardening. Recognizing these interrelated clinical features and the broader subtype aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, early detection, and targeted management of patients with arterial sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1932
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي (arteriosclerosis) is linked clinically to several findings and risk factors, including النفخة الانقباضية (systolic murmur), تعاطي التَبغ (tobacco use), حفظ الصحة الروتيني (routine health maintenance), عرج (limp), and لغط فخذي (thigh bruit). It is also associated with ذَبْهَةُ برنزميتال, indicating a broader clinical relationship. In the disease hierarchy, التصلب العصيدي is classified as a child of التصلب العصيدي المعمم (generalized arteriosclerosis) and as a descendant of مرض قلبي إقفاري مزمن، غير محدد (chronic unspecified ischemic heart disease). Vascular ischemia is reported to have a direct relationship with التصلب العصيدي, underscoring the role of arterial narrowing in ischemic processes. Additionally, التصلب العصيدي is related to مجازة الشريان التاجي (coronary artery graft), reflecting its relevance in coronary revascularization strategies. Together, these interconnections highlight the multifaceted clinical presentation, etiological factors, and therapeutic considerations associated with arteriosclerotic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1448
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is a clinical symptom that can be associated with تضيق الأبهر (aortic stenosis), a cardiac condition characterized by narrowing of the aortic valve and resulting hemodynamic compromise, as well as with داء المبيضات الفموي (oral thrush), an infection of the oral mucosa caused by Candida species. While تضيق الأبهر represents a direct cardiovascular cause of chest discomfort, داء المبيضات الفموي may reflect systemic or immunologic factors that can also manifest with chest pain, highlighting that nonspecific chest pain may arise from both cardiac and non‑cardiac origins. Recognizing this dual association is important for clinicians to conduct a thorough evaluation and to tailor management strategies appropriately.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 168
retrieved_docs.content: Atherosclerosis (التصلب العصيدي) is described as a chronic cardiovascular disorder affecting peripheral, visceral, abdominal aortic, and coronary arteries. In the abdominal aorta it often co‑exists with aortic calcification (تكلس أبهري) and can impair cerebral perfusion, producing a risk of cerebral hypoperfusion. Coronary atherosclerosis is classified under chronic ischemic heart disease and may present without angina, involve the pericardium (pericarditis الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر), or lead to acute subendocardial infarction. Clinical correlates include a systolic murmur (النفخة الانقباضية), carotid artery bruit (لغط الشريان السباتي), femoral artery bruit (لغط فخذي), abnormal electrocardiogram (مخطط كهربية القلب غير طبيعي), limp or gait disturbance (عرج), and the need for below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة). Routine health maintenance (حفظ الصحة الروتيني) and the condition ذَبْحَةُ برنزميتال are also linked to the disease spectrum. Inflammatory variants such as senile arteritis

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2914
retrieved_docs.content: Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is clinically associated with cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية). In clinical practice, the presence of chest pain often prompts consideration of cardiac catheterization to evaluate coronary artery disease, while the procedure itself may produce chest discomfort as a post‑procedural symptom. Recognizing this association helps clinicians diagnose underlying cardiac pathology and manage patient symptoms before, during, and after the intervention.
